author: Corpus Analyst Rowan-C

dimensionSummaries.dimension.player-fantasy: The player is one member of a deliberately interdependent three-hero team whose success depends on role sharing, vertical formation, and lightweight communication.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.core-loop: The cadence is to form a team, choose a costume, enter a short multi-stage course, coordinate puzzles and combat, defeat its climax, and use earned materials to prepare another role.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.verbs: Individual movement, attacks, and items combine with picking up, carrying, throwing, stacking into a totem, switching heroes in solo play, and sending communication icons.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.world-state: Within a run, three positions, shared health, held items, formation order, room completion, and special-mission conditions form a coupled team state reset at course boundaries.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.topology: The Drablands are organized as discrete themed areas, courses, and compact staged rooms whose exits require the whole team to satisfy local dependencies.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.gating-revisit: Course completion opens later content, materials enable costumes, and optional special missions invite returns under altered constraints rather than relying on a seamless overworld.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.puzzle-information: Height, color, item distribution, simultaneous positions, enemy placement, and teammate icons expose who must move, carry, throw, or act next.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.dungeon-structure: Each course is a bounded sequence of short cooperative puzzle-combat stages followed by a completion reward, with area themes escalating a small set of team rules.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.exploration-reward: Treasure and materials are awarded through courses and special conditions, making replay and mastery more important than freeform geographic searching.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.growth-economy: Collected materials are exchanged for costumes that alter abilities or team roles, so build choice changes tactics without a long numerical level curve.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.combat-boss: Enemies and bosses require shared health management, vertical reach, item-role coordination, timing, and recovery when another player controls a needed position.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.npc-quest: The kingdom's curse provides the campaign request, while the castle lobby, costume makers, and service characters organize teams, crafting, missions, and rewards.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.narrative-performance: The comic rescue premise stays light, allowing teamwork itself—waiting, signaling, carrying, mistiming, and celebrating—to become the dominant performed drama.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.camera-blocking: A high oblique view keeps all three heroes and vertical totem levels in frame, while compact stages reduce the chance that a required teammate acts beyond readable space.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.teaching-feedback: Totem height, role position, item effects, shared hearts, icon reactions, stage gates, and costume previews visibly explain what the team changed and what remains unresolved.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.ui-input: Each player receives ordinary action controls plus touch-screen communication icons; shared health and teammate status prioritize team information over a single-avatar HUD.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.recovery-accessibility: Shared health permits teammates to compensate for mistakes, course-scale restarts bound loss, and solo play replaces absent people with switchable invulnerable dolls.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.perspective-implementation: The 2.5D overhead-oblique camera makes horizontal separation and three-level stacking legible but favors compact authored rooms over free camera exploration.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.hardware-multiplayer: The original supports three-player local, download, and online cooperation plus solo switching; online cooperative service ended in 2024, making mode provenance important.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.production-runtime: Implementation requires synchronized three-actor state, stacking hierarchies, ownership transfer, shared health, role-specific items, icon communication, networking, reconnection policy, and solo AI-safe stand-ins.

observationThemes.context: A high target, enemy, switch, or route cannot be reached by one hero standing on the floor.
preconditions: Two or three heroes are present
The team can approach and form a stack
A player can control the base and another the active upper role
playerInput: Pick up one or more teammates
Have the appropriate level use an item or attack
before: The target lies outside individual reach
The team occupies separate ground positions
action: Assemble a two- or three-level totem and coordinate movement with the acting layer
after: The upper hero reaches the target height
The switch, enemy, or route can be affected
feedback: The stacked order and height are visually explicit
Target response confirms whether the correct layer and role acted
consequences: Progress depends on cooperation rather than three parallel solo actions
The same verb works with remote players, nearby players, or switchable solo stand-ins
observationThemes.factualDescription: Heroes can carry and stack one another into a totem; the base controls movement while an upper hero can reach elevated targets with attacks or items.
observationThemes.interpretation: Turning team membership into a visible spatial resource creates an immediately readable cooperative verb with distinct movement and action responsibilities.

observationThemes.context: During a cooperative room, heroes share health while different positions or items must act in a sequence that cannot be explained through free text chat.
preconditions: The next action depends on teammate timing or position
playerInput: Wait, move, carry, or use the assigned item in response
before: The team intent is not aligned
A mistake by any member can reduce the shared health pool
action: Use a bounded signal and coordinate the coupled team state
after: Players align around the requested action or reveal disagreement
The room dependency advances if coordination succeeds
feedback: Shared hearts, avatar reactions, and gate changes show collective result
consequences: Communication and recovery become part of the recurring stage cadence
Team behavior generates moment-to-moment dramatic tension without additional scripted dialogue
observationThemes.factualDescription: Cooperative play uses a shared health pool and touch-screen communication icons while rooms assign different movement or item responsibilities to the three players.
observationThemes.interpretation: Limited but expressive signals can focus collaboration on actionable intent, while shared consequences make even small misunderstandings socially meaningful.

observationThemes.context: After completing courses or special missions, the player has materials that can be exchanged for a costume with a stated ability change.
preconditions: A material reward has been collected
playerInput: Choose a recipe and spend materials
Equip the costume before a course
before: Collected materials have not been converted into a tactical option
action: Complete the crafting exchange and select the resulting role modifier
after: Its advertised ability changes relevant actions during play
feedback: Recipe requirements and costume preview expose the exchange
Changed attack, item, defense, or support behavior confirms the effect
consequences: Course replay yields new team compositions rather than only completion marks
Players can prepare complementary roles for later challenges
observationThemes.factualDescription: Course rewards include materials that service characters exchange for costumes, and equipped costumes alter combat, item, defense, or support capabilities.
observationThemes.interpretation: A crafting economy can turn replay rewards into legible team roles when each unlock changes a verb instead of merely increasing an opaque number.

observationThemes.context: A themed course advances through compact stages whose exits require every hero before culminating in a coordinated combat exam.
preconditions: Three controllable heroes and the stage's distributed items are present
playerInput: Bring the full team to the exit
Coordinate formation and items against the boss
before: Later course stages and the completion reward are locked
The local cooperative rules have not been fully tested
action: Clear the ordered stage chain and synthesize its rules in the final encounter
after: A reward and later route or challenge becomes available
feedback: Compact camera framing, stage transitions, and exit checks show group progress
Boss reactions expose effective role timing
consequences: The campaign advances through a clear course graph
Special missions can reuse the course with altered constraints for revisits
observationThemes.factualDescription: Courses consist of sequential cooperative puzzle-combat stages that require the team to reach their exits and conclude with a coordinated climax or boss before rewards are issued.
observationThemes.interpretation: Short bounded stages reduce coordination ambiguity and let a cooperative rule be introduced, combined, and examined before players commit to another course.

observationThemes.context: In single-player mode, a room still requires three bodies even though only one human is present.
preconditions: Three hero bodies exist as one controlled hero and two dolls
playerInput: Use the active hero to perform the next dependency
before: The required body or item is not under direct control
Inactive dolls occupy safe persistent positions
action: Transfer control and stage the three bodies sequentially
after: The newly active body performs its role
The room can satisfy the same positional rule used in multiplayer
feedback: The compact oblique camera keeps all bodies and stack levels readable
Control highlighting and invulnerable inactive dolls clarify responsibility
consequences: The campaign remains playable without partners
Runtime logic must preserve multiplayer dependencies while preventing inactive bodies from creating unfair failure
observationThemes.factualDescription: Solo play retains three hero bodies, lets the player switch among them, and keeps inactive dolls from taking damage while they are carried or positioned for puzzles.
observationThemes.interpretation: A solo fallback can preserve a cooperative spatial grammar by serializing roles and protecting inactive stand-ins, though the resulting cadence differs from live teamwork.

contributionHypotheses: A cooperative game's clearest signature verb can convert player count into an immediately visible spatial resource.
Bounded communication works best when every signal maps to a small set of observable team actions and consequences.
Solo fallback can retain multiplayer puzzle topology by serializing control, but it should be evaluated as a distinct pacing variant rather than assumed equivalent.
uniqueOrNonportable: The Hytopia setting, curse premise, named costumes, exact totem appearance, characters, stage layouts, enemy and boss designs, icon art, music, and dialogue are nonportable.
Exact recipes, material tables, course sequences, special-mission conditions, and boss solutions remain evidence only.
The retired Nintendo 3DS online service must not be represented as a currently available runtime feature.
openQuestions: Future benchmarking should compare live three-player comprehension with solo serial-control comprehension instead of merging their pacing evidence.
Current reproducibility should distinguish local/download play from the online cooperative service that ended on 2024-04-09.
